Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I work part-time to help cover my expenses, so I usually had to squeeze in studying whenever I could find a free moment. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd try to practice problems or physically write things out, but finding consistent time was really hard. Most weeks I'd manage maybe 10 hours if I was lucky, usually late at night after work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on everything else going on in my life. When I had work shifts or family obligations, studying got pushed aside completely. I'd try to catch up before deadlines, but it was more reactive than planned. I wished I could have a regular schedule, but my circumstances just didn't allow for that kind of consistency.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got a notification. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something, and that would remind me to check. I wasn't in the habit of just browsing around the platform regularly—it was more like panic-driven access when I realized I was falling behind on something.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a rhythm with the LMS. There were a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but then life got in the way and I lost that momentum. The platform never felt natural to me—I'm more of a hands-on learner, so sitting and clicking through online content felt disconnected from how I actually learn best.
Q5: The peer interactions were probably the most helpful thing for me. I connected with about 39 other students throughout the course, and talking through problems with them made concepts click in a way that videos didn't. When I could actually discuss things and work through examples together, that kinesthetic back-and-forth helped me understand. The videos were okay but I'd zone out watching them.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums beyond that one post I made. They felt performative, like I was just writing to check a box rather than actually learning. I also didn't engage much with the longer video lectures—I'd start watching but my attention would drift after about 20 minutes. Reading-heavy resources were hard for me too since I retain information better when I'm physically doing something.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or when it involved actually working through problems. If I could practice and make mistakes and learn from them, that was valuable. Anything that felt like busywork or passive consumption just didn't stick with me, especially when I had so little time to spare.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what was required just to stay afloat. Required activities at least had deadlines that forced me to engage, even if it was last-minute. Optional stuff, no matter how potentially useful, always got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually never happened.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied from printed notes because I can't afford constant internet access at home—I rely on campus wifi or my phone data which runs out. I'd print things at the library and study from paper, writing out problems by hand. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and worked through chapters that way.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS would show nothing. I'd be at my kitchen table working through printed problem sets for hours, or I'd meet up with classmates in person to go over material together. None of that showed up anywhere online. My actual learning happened mostly offline because that's what my situation required and how I learn best.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video to watch later when I had wifi, and then I'd spend days working through that material offline. The LMS would only show that one download, but I might have reviewed that same document five or six times on paper. My engagement looked minimal online but the actual studying was happening in my notebook.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. I was scared—genuinely scared—about failing, but I also had increased work hours and family stuff demanding my attention. My stress was through the roof and I just felt paralyzed. I knew I should be studying more but I couldn't bring myself to face how far behind I'd gotten. It was a really dark period.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often chose between studying and earning money to pay rent, and rent usually won. The confusion from missing classes made the LMS content harder to follow, which made me avoid it more. It became a vicious cycle.
Q14: Yes, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else, worrying about bills or work schedules. I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the words. My body was technically engaging with the LMS but my brain was checked out. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments because they felt overwhelming when I was already behind. I knew they were important but starting them meant confronting how much I didn't understand, and that scared me. It was easier to do small tasks or nothing at all than to face that anxiety. Procrastination was my way of protecting myself from feeling like a failure, even though it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage with the LMS. I'd cram right before something was due, which is why my assignment completion was so spotty—I'd miss some entirely because I didn't manage my time well. The deadline pressure helped me focus but it also meant I was always stressed and never really learning deeply, just surviving.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback, and when I did, it sometimes came too late to help me improve on the next thing. When I did receive encouraging feedback, it lifted my mood and made me want to try harder. But negative feedback or low grades just confirmed my fears that I wasn't cut out for this, which made me want to disengage more.
Q18: The structure assumed students had consistent time and internet access, which I didn't. Weekly modules with multiple components felt impossible to keep up with given my schedule. I would have done better with more flexibility or fewer but more substantial assignments. The rigid structure made me feel like I was constantly failing rather than making progress.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content was hard for me as a kinesthetic learner—I needed more interactive, hands-on activities. The peer support intervention actually helped a lot because connecting with other students gave me that interactive element. But the overall design didn't accommodate different learning styles or students with limited resources and unpredictable schedules.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. They might be clicking around, attempting to understand, but not confident enough to submit anything. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety that makes them engage superficially without being able to commit to completing assignments. It's like being present but paralyzed.
Q21: That somewhat reflects my experience, though I didn't perform well in the end. My low LMS activity didn't capture the offline studying I did or the peer conversations that helped me understand concepts. Someone could definitely learn a lot outside the system and still do okay on exams. The LMS only sees one slice of how people actually learn.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and for how long, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was actually learning or just had a tab open. They definitely miss offline studying, peer conversations, and the emotional state behind the numbers. A scared student cramming looks the same as a confident student reviewing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm actively working through problems, discussing ideas with peers, and feeling like I'm making progress. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time logged. This semester, I wanted to be engaged but fear and circumstances kept getting in the way.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was throughout this course, or how hard I was trying despite what my numbers look like. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from printed materials at home, or the support I got from classmates in person. It also wouldn't show that the peer support intervention genuinely helped my mood and made me feel less alone, even if my grades didn't reflect that improvement.
